name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمَآ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٠٣)
```

```english
Yet most people will not have faith, however eager you should be. (103)
```

```arabic
وَمَا تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٤)
```

```english
You do not ask them any reward for it: it is just a reminder for all the nations. (104)
```

```arabic
وَكَأَيِّن مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهَا وَهُمْ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضُونَ (١٠٥)
```

```english
How many a sign there is in the heavens and the earth that they pass by while they are disregardful of it! (105)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِٱللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ (١٠٦)
```

```english
And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him. (106)
```

```arabic
أَفَأَمِنُوٓا۟ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمْ غَـٰشِيَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٠٧)
```

```english
Do they feel secure from being overtaken by a blanket punishment from Allah, or being overtaken by the Hour, suddenly, while they are unaware? (107)
```

```arabic
قُلْ هَـٰذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ أَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا۠ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِى ۖ وَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٠٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’ (108)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ ۗ أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۗ وَلَدَارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٠٩)
```

```english
We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men from among the people of the towns, to whom We revealed. Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? And the abode of the Hereafter is surely better for those who are Godwary. Do you not exercise your reason? (109)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ٱسْتَيْـَٔسَ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا۟ جَآءَهُمْ نَصْرُنَا فَنُجِّىَ مَن نَّشَآءُ ۖ وَلَا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُنَا عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١١٠)
```

```english
When the apostles lost hope and they thought that they had been told lies, Our help came to them, and We delivered whomever We wished, and Our punishment will not be averted from the guilty lot. (110)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ كَانَ فِى قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ ۗ مَا كَانَ حَدِيثًا يُفْتَرَىٰ وَلَـٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١١١)
```

```english
There is certainly a moral in their accounts for those who possess intellect. This [Quran] is not a fabricated discourse; rather, it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of all things, and a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. (111)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses concluding the Surah mention that complete faith (الإيمان), which is pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — is difficult to attain; none achieve it except a very small minority of people. As for the majority, they are not believers, even if you were to crave their faith and exert your utmost effort in that regard. Even the minority who are believers possess only a faith tainted by polytheism (الشرك); thus, pure faith and sincere monotheism remain the portion of only a very few.

This pure monotheism is the path (سبيل) of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to which he calls based on clear insight (بصيرة), both he and those who follow him. Allah is his helper and the savior of those believers who follow him from the perils that threaten their monotheism and faith, and from the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) that will strike the polytheists, as has been the custom (عادة) of Allah with His past prophets, as is evident from their stories.

In their stories, there is a lesson (عبرة), an exposition of truths, and guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and mercy for the believers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **And most of the people will not believe, though you be eager [that they should].** (12:103). That is, it is not the nature of most people to believe, due to their devotion to the world, the attraction of their souls to its adornment, and their neglect of the knowledge of Allah and His signs (آيات) implanted in their primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — even if you were eager and desired their faith. The evidence for this meaning lies in the following verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا تَسْأَلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعَالَمِينَ** — **You do not ask them any reward for it: it is just a reminder for all the nations.** (12:104). The "and" (الواو) is circumstantial (حالية), meaning: they are not believers despite the fact that you do not ask them for any reward (أجر) for their faith or for this Quran which We reveal to you and which you recite to them, such that a financial penalty (الغرامة المالية) or the spending of wealth they love might hinder them from accepting his call and believing in him.

His saying: **إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعَالَمِينَ** is an exposition of the actual status of the Quran: that it is purely a reminder (ذكر) for all the worlds (العالمين) — *all creation* — by which they are reminded of the knowledge of Him and His signs that Allah has deposited in the hearts of human groups. It is nothing but a reminder by which they recall what heedlessness (الغفلة) and turning away (الإعراض) caused them to forget; it is not a commodity by which wealth is acquired, or through which might, status, or anything else is attained.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ آيَةٍ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهَا وَهُمْ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضُونَ** — **How many a sign there is in the heavens and the earth which they pass by while they are disregardful of it!** (12:105). The "and" (الواو) is circumstantial, though it may also be introductory (الاستئناف). "Passing by" (المرور) something means reaching it and then leaving it to reach what lies beyond it. Thus, passing by the celestial and terrestrial signs means witnessing them one after another.

The meaning is that there are many signs, celestial and terrestrial, which by their existence and the exquisite order (النظام البديع) prevailing within them, point to the oneness of their Lord. They witness these signs one after another, repeatedly, yet they are turning away (معرضون) from them, failing to take heed.

If His saying: **يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهَا** (they pass by them) were taken literally rather than metaphorically, it would serve as evidence for the basis of modern astronomy regarding the earth's rotational (وضعا) and orbital (انتقالا) motion. For it is we who pass by the celestial bodies through the earth's orbital and rotational movements, not the reverse as it appears to us in outward perception (ظاهر الحس).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ** — **And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him.** (12:106). The pronoun in "most of them" (أكثرهم) refers to the people in terms of their faith. That is, most people are not believers even though you ask them for no reward, and even though they pass by the celestial and terrestrial signs despite their abundance. As for those among them who do believe—and they are the minority—most of them do not believe in Allah except while they are clad in polytheism (الشرك).

A person being clad in both faith and polytheism simultaneously—despite them being two opposing concepts (معنيين متقابلين) that do not coexist in a single locus—is similar to being clad in other contradictory beliefs or opposing character traits. This occurs because these are concepts that inherently accept strength and weakness, thus varying by relation (النسبة) and comparison (الإضافة). This is like "nearness" and "distance"; while absolute nearness and absolute distance do not coexist, they are not prevented from coexisting and coinciding when they are relative. For example, Mecca is near in relation to Medina but distant in relation to Sham. Similarly, it is distant from Sham when compared to Medina, but near to it when compared to Baghdad.

Faith in Allah and polytheism regarding Him—the reality of which is the heart's attachment to Allah through submission to the Necessary Reality (الحقيقة الواجبية), versus the heart's attachment to other than Allah (the Exalted) who possesses nothing except by His permission—vary according to relation and comparison. It is possible for a person, for instance, to be attached to the fleeting worldly life and its false adornment, forgetting every truth and reality. Conversely, it is possible to detach from everything that diverts the soul and occupies it away from Allah (the Glorified), turning entirely toward Him, remembering Him and never being heedless of Him, relying in one's essence and attributes only upon Him, and desiring only what He desires, like the sincere (المخلصين) among His friends (أولياء).

Between these two stations are various ranks characterized by proximity to or distance from either side. These are the ranks in which the two sides coexist in some manner of combination. Evidence for this includes the character traits and qualities established in souls that contradict the requirements of what they believe, whether true or false, as well as the actions proceeding from them. You see one who claims faith in Allah yet fears and trembles at any misfortune or calamity that threatens him, while he remembers that "there is no power except with Allah." He seeks might and status from others while he recites His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** — **"Indeed all might belongs to Allah"** (10:65). He knocks on every door seeking sustenance (الرزق) which Allah has guaranteed, and he disobeys Allah without shame while he sees that his Lord is aware of what is in his soul, hears what he says, and sees what he does, and that nothing is hidden from Him in the earth or in the heaven, and so on.

The intended meaning of polytheism (الشرك) in the verse is some of its levels that can coexist with some levels of faith, which is termed in the science of ethics (فن الأخلاق) as "hidden polytheism" (الشرك الخفي).

Therefore, what has been said—that the "polytheists" intended in the verse are the polytheists of Mecca—is misplaced. Likewise, the claim that they are the hypocrites (المنافقون) is a restriction of the verse's absolute (إطلاق) meaning without a restrictor (مقيد).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَأَمِنُوا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ غاشِيَةٌ مِنْ عَذابِ اللهِ أَوْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ السَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ** — **Do they feel secure from the envelopment of Allah’s punishment overtaking them, or the sudden visitation of the Hour while they are unaware?** (12:107). "The envelopment" (الغاشية) is an adjective standing in place of a deleted noun because the word "punishment" (عذاب) indicates it; the estimation is: "an enveloping punishment" (عقوبة غاشية) that covers and surrounds them.

"Suddenly" (بغتة) means unexpectedly. His saying: **وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ** (while they are unaware) is a circumstantial clause referring to the plural pronoun; that is, the Hour surprises them in its coming while they do not perceive its arrival because it is not preceded by signs that specify its time or identify its occurrence. The interrogative is for expressing wonder (التعجيب). The meaning is: their state in turning away from the signs of heaven and earth, their lack of sincere faith in Allah, and their persistence in heedlessness is wondrous. Do they feel secure from a punishment from Allah that envelops them, or an Hour that surprises and stuns them?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحانَ اللهِ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — **Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight, I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’** (12:108). Since He (the Glorified) mentioned that pure faith in Him and sincere monotheism for Him is difficult to attain—and it is the manifest truth to which the signs of the heavens and earth point—He commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to explain to them that his path is the call to this monotheism based on insight.

Thus, His saying: **هذِهِ سَبِيلِي** (This is my way) is an announcement of his path. His saying: **أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ** (I summon to Allah with insight) is an exposition of that path. His saying: **وَسُبْحانَ اللهِ** (Immaculate is Allah) is a parenthetical glorification (تنزيه). His saying: **وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** (and I am not one of the polytheists) is a confirmation of the meaning of the call to Allah and an explanation that this call is not a call to Him in just any manner, but a call based on pure monotheism from which there is no deviation toward polytheism at all.

As for His saying: **أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي** (I and he who follows me), it is an expansion and generalization of the responsibility of the call; although the path is the path of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and specific to him, the carrying of the call and rising for it is not restricted to him. Rather, whoever follows him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) carries it out for himself.

However, the context indicates that "following" is not as broad as the literal wording of "he who follows me" might suggest. For the path defined by the verse is the call based on insight and certainty (يقين) to pure faith and sincere monotheism. Only one who is sincere to Allah in his religion, knowledgeable of his Lord's station, and possessing insight and certainty can share with him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in this. Not everyone of whom it can be said that they "followed him" fits this description. Nor is standing upon this level granted to every believer, even those whom Allah (the Glorified) counted in the previous verse among the polytheists and censured for being heedless of their Lord, feeling secure from His scheme, and turning away from His signs. How can one call to Allah who is heedless of Him, feels secure from His scheme, and turns away from His signs and remembrance? Allah has described those with such attributes in many verses as being in misguidance, blindness, and loss, and these traits never coexist with guidance and direction.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرَى** — **We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men from the people of the towns, to whom We revealed.** (12:109) to the end of the verse. When He (the Glorified) mentioned the state of the people regarding faith in Him, and then the state of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his calling them based on a divine message without asking them for a reward or seeking personal benefit, He explained that this is not an unprecedented matter (بدع من الأمر). Rather, it is what the divine custom (السنة الإلهية) has followed in religious calling. The past messengers were not angels; they were sent from among these people. They were men from the people of the towns (أهل القرى) who mingled with the people and were known to them. Allah revealed to them and sent them toward the people to call them to Him, just as the Prophet is. It is possible for those being called to travel through the land and see what was the end of those before them; their ruined cities and empty dwellings speak eloquently of what their affair became, and inform of the consequence of their disbelief, denial, and rejection of Allah's signs.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only calls them as the prophets before him called them, and he only calls them to what contains their good and the rectitude of their state: that they should be God-conscious (يتقوا الله) so they may succeed and attain eternal happiness and lasting bliss in an abiding abode. "And the abode of the Hereafter is better for those who are God-conscious. Do you not exercise your reason?"

Thus, His saying: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرَى** is an application of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) call to the call of the messengers before him. Perhaps describing them as being "from the people of the towns" is to indicate that they were from among themselves, living among them and known to them through social interaction and mingling; they were not angels nor from other than themselves. This is supported by describing them as "men" (رجالا), for men were closer to being known than women who remained behind veils.

His saying: **أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** — **Have they not travelled through the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them?** (12:109) is a warning to the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) community similar to what the past nations were warned with, but they did not listen and thus tasted the evil consequence of their affair.

His saying: **وَلَدَارُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ** — **And the abode of the Hereafter is surely better for those who are God-conscious. Do you not exercise your reason?** (12:109) is an expression of sincere advice: that what they are called to—which is godwariness (التقوى)—leads to nothing but their total good and the sum of their happiness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حَتَّى إِذَا اسْتَيْأَسَ الرُّسُلُ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا جَاءَهُمْ نَصْرُنَا** — **Until when the apostles lost hope and thought that they had been called liars, Our help came to them.** (12:110) to the end of the verse. It has been mentioned that "despaired" (يأس) and "lost hope" (استيئس) carry the same meaning. However, it is not far-fetched to say that *istiy'as* (استيئس) denotes approaching despair through the appearance of its signs, due to the linguistic form *istif'al* (استفعال); it is what is conventionally considered despair, though not necessarily absolute, actual despair.

His saying: **حَتَّى إِذَا اسْتَيْأَسَ** etc., the limit (الغاية) relates to what is understood from the previous verse. The meaning is: those messengers who were men like you from the people of the towns, and those are their perished towns—they called them but they did not respond, and they warned them of Allah's punishment but they did not desist, until when the messengers lost hope in the faith of those people, and the people thought that the messengers had been lied to—meaning they had been falsely informed of the punishment—Our help came. Thus, We save whom We will, and they are the believers, and Our might (بأسنا)—meaning Our severity—is not turned back from the guilty people (القوم المجرمين).

As for the messengers losing hope in their people's faith, it is as reported in the story of Noah: **وَأُوحِيَ إِلَى نُوحٍ أَنَّهُ لَنْ يُؤْمِنَ مِنْ قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ** — **"It was revealed to Noah: ‘None of your people will believe except those who have already believed’"** (11:36); and: **وَقَالَ نُوحٌ رَبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبَادَكَ وَلَا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فَاجِراً كَفَّاراً** — **"And Noah said, ‘My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. If You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and they will beget none but vicious ingrates’"** (71:26-27). Similar instances are found in the stories of Hud, Salih, Shu'ayb, Moses, and Jesus (peace be upon them).

As for their nations' thought that they had been lied to, it is as reported in the story of Noah from their saying: **بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كَاذِبِينَ** — **"rather we consider you to be liars"** (11:27). Likewise in the stories of Hud and Salih, and His saying: **فَقَالَ لَهُ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يَا مُوسَى مَسْحُوراً** — **"Pharaoh said to him, ‘O Moses, I indeed think you are bewitched’"** (17:101).

As for the saving of the believers through help, it is like His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَانَ حَقّاً عَلَيْنَا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **"and it was a must for Us to help the believers"** (30:47). He has also reported this regarding the destruction of some nations, such as His saying: **نَجَّيْنَا هُوداً وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ** — **"We delivered Hud and those who had faith with him"** (11:58); **نَجَّيْنَا صَالِحاً وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ** — **"We delivered Salih and those who had faith with him"** (11:66); **نَجَّيْنَا شُعَيْباً وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ** — **"We delivered Shu‘ayb and those who had faith with him"** (11:94), and others.

As for the fact that Allah's might is not turned back from the guilty, it is mentioned in many verses generally and specifically, such as His saying: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذَا جَاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ** — **"There is an apostle for every nation. When their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged"** (10:47); and His saying: **وَإِذَا أَرَادَ اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوءاً فَلَا مَرَدَّ لَهُ وَمَا لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَالٍ** — **"And when Allah wishes to visit ill on a people, there is nothing that can avert it, and they have no protector besides Him"** (13:11), among other verses.

This is the best of the meanings they have provided for the verse, and the evidence for it is that the verse's content serves as the limit (غاية) for what its predecessor contains, as we previously explained. They have provided other meanings for it, none of which are free from defect, and turning away from them is more appropriate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ كَانَ فِي قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ لِأُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ** — **There is certainly a moral in their stories for those who possess intellect.** (12:111) to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "The root of *'ibrah* (عبر) is crossing from one state to another. As for *'ubur* (العبور), it is specific to crossing water... and *i'tibar* (الاعتبار) and *'ibrah* (العبرة) refer to the state through which one reaches from the knowledge of the observed to that which is not observed. He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّ فِي ذَلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً** (Indeed in that is a lesson)." [End of quote].

The pronoun in "their stories" (قصصهم) refers to the prophets, including Joseph, the subject of the story in the Surah. It is also possible that it refers to Joseph and his brothers. The meaning is: I swear, there was in the stories of the prophets, or Joseph and his brothers, a lesson for those of intellect. The stories mentioned in the Surah were not a fabricated discourse (حديثا يفتري), but a confirmation of what was before the Quran, namely the Torah in which the story is mentioned—meaning the Torah of Moses (peace be upon him).

His saying: **وَتَفْصِيلَ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** (and an elaboration of all things) etc., means an exposition and distinction of everything that people need in their religion, upon which the foundation of their happiness in this world and the Hereafter is built. It is a guidance to happiness and success, and a special mercy from Allah (the Glorified) for a people who believe in Him, for it is a mercy from Allah to them, by which they are guided through His guidance to a straight path (صراط مستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah*.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of transmission from al-Fudayl, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ** (And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him). He said: **شرك طاعة وليس شرك عبادة ، والمعاصي التي يرتكبون فهي شرك طاعة ـ أطاعوا فيها الشيطان فأشركوا بالله الطاعة لغيره ـ وليس بإشراك عبادة أن يعبدوا غير الله** — "It is the polytheism of obedience, not the polytheism of worship. The sins they commit are polytheism of obedience—they obeyed Satan in them, thus associating others with Allah in obedience—it is not the polytheism of worship such that they would worship other than Allah."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Muhammad bin al-Fudayl, from al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: **شرك لا يبلغ به الكفر** — "It is a polytheism that does not reach the level of disbelief (الكفر)."

In the same source, from Malik bin Atiyyah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: **هو الرجل يقول : لو لا فلان لهلكت ـ ولو لا فلان لأصبت كذا وكذا ولضاع عيالي ـ ألا ترى أنه جعل لله شريكا في ملكه يرزقه ويدفع عنه؟ قال : قلت : فيقول : لو لا أن من الله علي بفلان لهلكت ـ قال : نعم لا بأس بهذا** — "It is the man who says: 'Had it not been for so-and-so, I would have perished,' and 'Had it not been for so-and-so, I would have obtained such-and-such, and my family would have been lost.' Do you not see that he has assigned to Allah a partner in His kingdom who provides for him and defends him?" He [the narrator] said: I asked: "So should he say: 'Had it not been that Allah favored me with so-and-so, I would have perished'?" He replied: "Yes, there is no harm in this."

In the same source, from Zurarah, he said: I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: **وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللَّهِ ـ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ**. He said: **من ذلك قول الرجل : لا وحياتك** — "Among that is a man's saying: 'No, by your life!'"

**I say:** He means swearing by other than Allah because it involves venerating the object of the oath in a manner it does not inherently deserve. The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Sallam bin al-Mustanir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي**. He said: **ذاك رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وأمير المؤمنين ـ والأوصياء من بعدهما** — "That is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the Commander of the Faithful, and the Successors (الأوصياء) after them."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Abu Amr al-Zubayri, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: **يعني على أول من اتبعه على الإيمان والتصديق له ـ وبما جاء به من عند الله عز وجل من الأمة التي بعث فيها ومنها ـ وإليها قبل الخلق ممن لم يشرك بالله قط ـ ولم يلبس إيمانه بظلم وهو الشرك** — "It means [based on the insight of] the first of those who followed him in faith and in attesting to him and to what he brought from Allah (Mighty and Majestic is He), from the community in which he was sent, from it, and to it, before [the rest of] creation—those who never associated anything with Allah and never obscured their faith with wrongdoing (ظلم), which is polytheism."

**I say:** These two narrations support what we previously presented in the commentary on the verse. There are other narrations with similar meanings, and perhaps the mention of specific individuals is by way of application (التطبيق).

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Hisham bin al-Hakam, he said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: **سُبْحانَ اللهِ** (Immaculate is Allah), what does He mean by it? He said: **أنفة لله** — "It is a disdain [for any deficiency attributed] to Allah."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Hisham al-Jawaliqi, he said: I asked Abu Abdillah about the saying of Allah (Mighty and Majestic is He): **سُبْحانَ اللهِ**. He said: **تنزيه** — "It is a glorification (تنزيه) — *the declaration of Allah's absolute purity from any likeness, partner, or deficiency*."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission from al-Sayyar, from al-Hasan bin Ali, from his fathers, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), in a tradition where he said while addressing someone: **أولست تعلم أن الله تعالى لم يخل الدنيا قط ـ من نبي أو إمام من البشر؟ أوليس الله تعالى يقول : « وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ » يعني إلى الخلق « إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى » فأخبر أنه لم يبعث الملائكة إلى الأرض فيكونوا أئمة وحكاما وإنما أرسلوا إلى الأنبياء** — "Do you not know that Allah (the Exalted) has never left the world without a prophet or an Imam from among humanity? Does Allah (the Exalted) not say: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ**—meaning to the creation—**إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرَى**? Thus He informed that He did not send angels to the earth to be Imams and rulers; they were only sent to the prophets."

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain of transmission from Ali bin Muhammad bin al-Jahm, he said: I attended the assembly of al-Ma'mun while al-Rida, Ali bin Musa (peace be upon him), was with him. Al-Ma'mun said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, is it not your view that the prophets are infallible (معصومون)?" He replied: "Yes." He mentioned the tradition until he said: Al-Ma'mun said to Abu al-Hasan: "Then inform me about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **حَتَّى إِذَا اسْتَيْأَسَ الرُّسُلُ ـ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا جَاءَهُمْ نَصْرُنَا**." Al-Rida said: **يقول الله : حتى إذا استيئس الرسل من قومهم ـ فظن قومهم أن الرسل قد كذبوا جاء الرسل نصرنا** — "Allah says: Until when the messengers lost hope in their people, and their people thought that the messengers had been lied to, Our help came to the messengers."

**I say:** This supports what we previously presented in the commentary on the verse. As for what is found in some narrations—that the messengers thought Satan had appeared to them in the form of angels—it is not to be relied upon.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Zurarah, he said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "How was it that the Messenger of Allah did not fear, regarding what came to him from Allah, that it might be from the promptings of Satan?" He said: **إن الله إذا اتخذ عبدا رسولا ـ أنزل عليه السكينة والوقار ـ وكان الذي يأتيه من الله مثل الذي يراه بعينه** — "Indeed, when Allah takes a servant as a messenger, He sends down upon him tranquility (السكينة) and dignity (الوقار), and what comes to him from Allah is like what he sees with his own eyes."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibrahim, from Abu Hamzah al-Jazari, who said: "I prepared food and invited some of our companions, among them Sa'id bin Jubayr and al-Dahhak bin Muzahim. A young man from Quraysh asked Sa'id bin Jubayr: 'O Abu Abdillah, how do you recite this word? For whenever I come across it, I wish I were not reciting this Surah: **حَتَّى إِذَا اسْتَيْأَسَ الرُّسُلُ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا**.' He replied: 'Yes, until when the messengers lost hope in their people that they would believe them, and those to whom they were sent thought that the messengers had been lied to.' Al-Dahhak then said: 'If you had traveled to Yemen for this [explanation], it would have been but a little [effort].'"
